meta.name: Public Services — Water
meta.department: Department of Water Supply (DWS) + Board of Water Supply
meta.description: govOS free public service: a plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 14 (Public Services), Article 1 Water — getting water service, meters, charges, paying your bill, and who pays. Sourced to Municode + Dept. of Water Supply; hands off to the County's billing portal. First version, expanding.
meta.pageTitle: Title 14 — Public Services (Water) · govOS · Kilo Aupuni
meta.eyebrow: 12 Stones Global · Kilo Aupuni · govOS · public service

hero.lead: A plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 14 (Public Services), Article 1 — Water: how to get water service, how metering and charges work, how to pay your bill, and who pays. Run by the Department of Water Supply (DWS); you manage and pay your account on the County's billing portal — this service explains the law and hands you off to the official system. Usage rates change with the annual budget; figures marked expanding are being filled in. Nothing here is legal advice.

sections.title: ★ Parcel lookup
sections.note: Water service depends on your location. Enter a Maui County TMK to see the parcel's designations from the Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Whether DWS can serve a parcel turns on being within service limits and adjacent to a distributing main with adequate supply — confirm a specific parcel's water availability with DWS Customer Service (270-7730).

source.html: Live designations: Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Water service & billing: Dept. of Water Supply. No parcel data is stored or fabricated — fetched live.

sections.title: 1 · Get water service
sections.note: How a new or transferred water account works under Ch. 14.04.

steps.name: Check availability
steps.what: DWS may provide service where the premises are within service limits, adjacent to a distributing main, pressure permits, and there is sufficient developed supply for domestic, irrigation, and fire protection without detriment to existing customers.
steps.pay: Expense: free to ask · paid by: you (time)

steps.name: Apply / open an account
steps.what: Call DWS Customer Service (270-7730, 1888 Wili Pa Loop, Wailuku) to open an account, start/stop service, or change address, and agree to abide by the County water code.
steps.pay: Expense: account setup · paid by: applicant

steps.name: Meter & connection
steps.what: All water is metered in gallons (a flat rate may apply where metering is impractical). DWS sets the location and size of meters and service connections; connections become DWS property for operation and maintenance.
steps.pay: Expense: meter/connection fees · paid by: applicant

steps.name: System improvements (if needed)
steps.what: Where system improvements aren't yet installed, the applicant must install and pay for the required storage tanks, appurtenances, and pipeline from the storage tank to the premises (Ch. 14.05).
steps.pay: Expense: storage + pipeline · paid by: applicant

steps.name: Get billed & pay
steps.what: You receive a monthly bill (meter service charge + usage). Pay online, by phone, or via Automatic Bill Payment.
steps.pay: Expense: monthly water/sewer · paid by: customer

position.label: ⚖️ Position — James Langford (analysis, not law; a stance beside the rule)
position.text: Water is life — a public trust resource. Where the County requires an applicant to build public water infrastructure (storage, mains) to get served, that cost-shift should be weighed against the County's duty to provide clean, plentiful water; the burden of extending the public system shouldn't fall entirely on the last resident to connect.

source.html: Maui County Code Ch. 14.04 (Water Service) incl. §14.04.060; Ch. 14.05 (improvements). County Department of Water Supply.

sections.title: 2 · Water charges
sections.note: Your bill has two parts: a monthly meter Water Service Charge (to read/maintain/replace your meter) plus usage. Usage rates are set in the County's annual budget ordinance.

source.html: County of Maui Water Charges; meter service charges $9.25 (5/8") to $650 (8"); usage per the annual rates & fees ordinance.

sections.title: 3 · Pay your water bill
sections.note: Manage and pay your DWS account online.
sections.html: Create an account to view bills, see account info, and go paperless. Pay online or by phone with credit card, debit card, electronic check, Apple Pay, or Google Pay; or set up Automatic Bill Payment (ABP) so your bank pays the water/sewer bill automatically on the due date. Questions, start/stop service, or address changes: DWS Customer Service 270-7730 (Mon–Fri, 8am–4pm).

source.html: County of Maui Pay Your Water Bill + Fiscal/Customer Service Division.

sections.title: 4 · Enforcement
sections.note: Water-code violations are reachable through the County enforcement engine. Not legal advice.
sections.html: Title 19's enforcement chapter, §19.530.030, expressly reaches violations of MCC titles 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 19 and 20 plus related rules and permits — so a Title 14 water violation (e.g. unauthorized connection or cross-connection) can draw a notice of violation and order with civil fines, with one carve-out the code itself names: unpaid civil fines may be added to County taxes or charges except residential water/sewer. DWS also enforces its own service rules directly.

source.html: Maui County Code Ch. 19.530 (§19.530.030 reach + the residential water/sewer carve-out, sourced in the Title 19 service) + DWS service rules.

sections.title: 5 · Who pays — the charter lens
sections.note: Labeled analysis — water service read through two charters. Not adopted law; an interpretive lens beside the Code.
sections.mauiCharter: Under the Maui County Charter, the Department of Water Supply (a semi-autonomous department with its own Board) provides water as a self-supporting utility — rates and fees are set to cover operation, maintenance, and capital, with the Charter directing the County to provide clean, healthful, and plentiful water.
sections.sovereignCharter: Under the Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) lens, water is a public-trust resource, not merely a commodity; the resident's claim to domestic water is a foundational right. The lens reads cost-recovery as legitimate for use, but treats forcing the last-connecting resident to fund the public main itself as the County offloading a public duty.

position.label: ⚖️ Position — analysis, not law
position.text: Charge fairly for the water a household uses; treat the backbone system (mains, source, storage) as the County's public duty under its own charge to provide plentiful water. Same service, read two ways.

sections.title: 6 · Rules → Code: candidates for codification
sections.intro: Provisions that, in our assessment, should be codified or made transparent in Title 14 so cost and duty are readable from the Code. Citations are verbatim-checked; the recommendation is a stance beside the rule.
items: Publish the usage-rate table in-Code. Usage rates live in the annual budget ordinance and shift yearly. → Require a plain-language tiered-rate table published with each rate change so residents can see the trend. expanding

Source-development cost sharing. Whether the applicant or the County funds new mains/storage is decided case-by-case. → Codify a cost-share rule so extending the public system isn't fully downloaded onto the newest connection.
Protect the residential water/sewer carve-out. The Code already shields residential water/sewer from tax-lien collection of fines — keep and clarify that protection in Title 14 itself.

source.html: Analysis grounded in MCC Title 14 + the County water rates framework; contents expanding pending verbatim ingest.

sections.text: Free now (no account): this page, the live parcel lookup, the service navigator, and the charges table are all Tier 1 — free. Coming — Tier 3 (verified tools): a water-service-availability checker and an AI water-account assistant will be verification-gated (identity-verified). Identity is verified through the payment processor — only verified-status is stored here, never the raw ID. No live billing is enabled (Stripe keys are environment references only); the actual water payment happens on the County's own billing portal.
